title: Closest Pair of Points in the Plane
whenToUse: Asked directly, or as a model for D&C with a 'clever linear-time combine'. Also a good analogue for 2D maxima / Pareto frontier design problems.
keyFormulas: Each point in ValidS_y needs only its next 15 neighbors checked (optimally: 6)
professor:
**Problem.** Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, find the minimum pairwise distance. Brute force is $O(n^2)$; D&C achieves $O(n \log n)$.

**One-time preprocessing (outside recursion).** Sort all points by $x$-coordinate into $S_x$ and by $y$-coordinate into $S_y$. $O(n \log n)$.

**Recursive algorithm FindClosest$(P)$.**

1. **Base case.** If $|P| \le 3$, brute force.
2. Split $P$ along the median $x$-coordinate $x^*$ into $P_L, P_R$.
3. $d_L \leftarrow$ FindClosest$(P_L)$; $d_R \leftarrow$ FindClosest$(P_R)$.
4. $d \leftarrow \min(d_L, d_R)$.
5. **Strip check.** Consider only points with $|x - x^*| < d$ ('the strip'). Restrict $S_y$ to these ('ValidS$_y$'). For each point in ValidS$_y$, compute distance to the next 15 points (in ValidS$_y$ order). Take the minimum.
6. Return $\min(d, \text{strip min})$.

**Four geometric claims justify the strip check.**

- **Claim 1.** If the closest across-pair is $(p_1, p_2)$ with $p_1 \in P_L, p_2 \in P_R$, then both have $x$-coordinate within $d$ of $x^*$. (Otherwise distance $> d$, contradicting $d = \min(d_L, d_R)$.)
- **Claim 2.** In the strip, tile it into $(d/2) \times (d/2)$ squares. No two points lie in the same square (diagonal length $= d/\sqrt{2} < d$, which would beat $d$ on one of the sides â€” contradicting $d_L, d_R \ge d$).
- **Claim 3.** If $(p_1, p_2)$ with $p_1$ above $p_2$ spans 3 or more whole rows of squares, $|y_1 - y_2| \ge 3d/2 > d$ â€” so they can't be the closest pair.
- **Claim 4.** Hence, if $(p_1, p_2)$ is the closest pair and both are in the strip, then $p_2$ is within the next 15 positions of $p_1$ in ValidS$_y$. (Pigeonhole: only 15 squares satisfy both 'not above $p_1$' and 'within 3 rows of $p_1$'; at most one point per square.)

**Runtime.** Each recursive call does $O(n)$ non-recursive work (splitting, computing ValidS$_y$ from $S_y$, checking 15 neighbors per point). Recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. Preprocessing is also $O(n \log n)$, total still $\Theta(n \log n)$.

**Why precompute $S_y$.** If you sorted by $y$ inside each recursive call, the combine would be $O(n \log n)$, giving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n = \Theta(n \log^2 n)$. Still better than $n^2$ but not optimal. Precomputing once buys $O(n)$ per combine.
simplified:
**Goal:** closest pair of $n$ points in 2D in $O(n \log n)$.

**Preprocess once:** sort by $x$ (get $S_x$), sort by $y$ (get $S_y$). $O(n \log n)$.

**Recursive step:**

1. Split by median $x$.
2. Recurse on each half, get $d_L, d_R$. Let $d = \min$.
3. Points within $\pm d$ of the median $x$ form 'the strip'. Their closest pair might beat $d$.
4. Walk through the strip sorted by $y$. For each point, check distance to the next 15 (in $y$ order). Take the min.
5. Return overall min.

**The magic fact:** in the strip, only 15 points can be 'close enough' to any given point to possibly beat $d$ â€” because the strip is narrow and points can't be too close (that'd contradict $d$), so they fit into a bounded number of small squares.

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$. Same as Merge Sort. $\Theta(n \log n)$.

**Pattern to steal:** 'D&C + exploit a bound from smaller subproblems to do a linear-time combine.' Generalizes to many D&C designs.
